headlines.title: A Fitting Advertisement for New Water Supply
headlines.fullText: While the various possible ways of marking the completion of the West Fork reservoir in a way that will call attention to the great size of the lake it creates are being considered, Fort Worth people might think about the advertising power of a big regatta held on the lake at the time the work is done. A series of boat races between champion single scullers would not only attract thousands of people to witness the matches but would be read about all over the country. When it became known that the world's rowing championship was to be settled on Lake Minnetonka, people in other places would realize as never before that Fort Worth by means of the dam had established within its reach a great inland lake. The expense of such a regatta would be comparatively small. Beyond the prizes there would be little necessary cost and that amount would be so small as to insure ample return from the investment. Such a celebration need not interfere with other celebrations. In fact, It could be' made but one of several features. Motorboat races and even hydroplanes might supplement the rowing events. The rowing events, however, could be made the subject of greatest advertisement to the city, provided the races arranged were really championship events -- ones that would settle the world's rowing laurels. In that event the news of the Fort Worth races would go to every part of the civilized world and everywhere they went Fort Worth's great water supply would become known. Estimates have been made that the dam will be completed within eight months at the outside. The design has been so made that storage of water begins long before completion of the work which is to go ahead fast enough to keep up with the rise of the water back of the dam. The lake will accordingly be ready when the dam is completed. Negotiations of length will be necessary to bring about such a rowing event as Fort Worth ought to have and it is not too soon to big take steps along along this line line. Nothing but a big event would be worth while, and if it is to be a big event, preliminary work looking to the matching of the possible title claimants should begin.

headlines.title: The Keokuk Dam
headlines.subtitle: II-THE ENGINEERING FEAT.
headlines.fullText: By Frederic J. Haskin. The big central fact about the greatest water power plant in the world is the dam itself, the colossal barrier of concrete that balts the Mississippi en Ita course to the gulf and diverts lis waters through a power house where their energy may be used to make electric power. No such dam ever was built before, no such river ever was halted before, there were no precedents and no guides to follow. IL was an engineering problem as difficult in its way as that at Panama, and one the solution of which can stand only second to that of the great canal. Here at Keokuk is the foot of the Des Moines Rapids, a place where nature had already done much to help the work, A glacier once dammed the wider course of the river to the west and then this channel was cut through in e valley much narrower so that the bluffs on both sides of the river are quite close together. In the twelve miles above Keokuk the water falls twenty-three feet, and it is these rapids that have always obstructed navigation in the upper Mississippi. To dam these rapids, practically a mile wide from shore to shore, and to dam them in a climate varying from 199 degrees above to 27 degrees below zero, to dam them under conditions where a winter bombardment of ice was to be expected and to be feared but a spring deluge, admittedly was a difficult undertaking. The power house was to be on the Iowa side. The dam started from the Illinois side, keyed into a limestone bluff and built out into the river under the protection of an advance coffer dam. Another coffer dam unwatered the bed of the river on the power house site, Including the abutments the dam is a mile long, but the dam itself, the 119 concrete spans, pier measure is 4,278 feet, or over four-fifths of a mile. Each pier is six feet thick, the distance between the pler-the inside measurement of the spans-is thirty feet. The viaduct that tops the dam structure is twenty-nine feet wide and the dam at the bottom of the river is forty-two feet wide. When these piers were finished the dam had the appearance of a many-spanned bridge, for there was nothing between the plers and they offered comparatively little obstruction to the river flow. The spillway between the piers were built in later. On the upper side they have a vertical face, but on the down stream side they are so curved down and out that their surface is just the shape of the under side of the water as it falls over the top of the spillway. These spillways were built gradually so as to offer the minimum resistance to the flow of the river, The work was done by bullding slowly from one side of the river to the other, and also by building slowly from the bottom up. Five feet of concrete was laid at first between two piers, the only obstruction then being one thirty-foot coffer dam in the whole stream. Span at a time was filled in in this way, until the bottom layer was finished. Then another five foot layer was built all across, span at a time, and so on until the last layers brought the top of the spillway up-river to thirty-two feet above the river bed. On the side of each span were slots let into the sides, and into the top of the spillway, in which slots alide 119 steel gates. These can be let down so as to form a complete obstruction to the river. These gates are 11 by 35 feet in dimension and will be rateod and lowered by electric power, each being operated singly from an electric traveling crane on top of the viaduct. These gates will keep the water above the dam at a constant level, never varying. In high water they will be opened and the surplus will flow through, In low water a current of them will be closed to maintain the desired level, Concrete is a mixture of sand, crushed rock, cement and water. This is the biggest concrete structure in the world and therefore the nature of this part of the work is of interest. The sand was found on the Illinois side only two miles below the dam, and no less than 300,000 euble yards of it were used. Loaded on wagons all at one time this would mean a procession of sand carts that would reach from New York through Denver to the western boundary of Colorado, Stone for the crushers was taken from the bluffs at both ends of the dam, largely stone that had to be excavated anyhow. The cement came up the river from Hannibal, sixty miles below. On account of the great range of temperature at this point, nice calculations had to be made of the probable effect on the cement structure. Expansion joints were set in the top of the dam, sheets of tar paper being suspended vertically across the structure inside the steel molds when the concrete was poured in. When this paper rots out there will be a narrow fissure which will pull apart in hot weather and close up in cold. Careful experiments showed that these expansion joints were necessary only for the surface work, as the concrete under water would be but little affected by the change in temperature. The actual work of construction was most difficult. The coffer dam built ahead of the dam sections was of cribs of logs, filled in with rock and faced with a seal of puddled clay. The log work was done by timber-jacks from Canada, men who were trained In the handling of heavy logs in swift water. A raft the size of the crib would be made and floated out to the place where the crib was to be sunk. Then careful measurements were made of the bottom of the river at that place and the bottom set of logs cut to fit. Then the logs w.te built up criss-cross, like a log house, and filled in. These dams leaked but little and made the use of the concrete comparatively easy. The first span was built from the Illinois shore and then each succeeding span worked out from that end. As the last few were always too soft to work on, a Huge traveling crane of the cantilever type was employed, a monster with an arm 150 feet long, from which it poured the concrete in the forms. When built It was the largest traveling crane in the world, and of it self was one of the chlet items in the more than a million dollars worth of tools used in the work on the power plant. The dam was built, of course, to create power, but as the Mississippi is a navigatable river it must be kept open. Therefore the power company agreed in consideration of permission to build the dam to keep open navigation end to substitute for the old government canal chat skirted the rapids on the fowa side a new tanal, lock and dry dock. This part of the work alone cost ever two million dollars. But as it assures a deep water stage for the more than wisty miles above the fam, its benefits will represent the earnings on a capitalization of perhaps ten millione-going directly to the navigators of the river. The lock here is larger even than those at Panama. being of equal width-110 feet-and of greater height. The lock chamber is much shorter, being only 400 feet inaide measurement, since the steam boats on the river do require such a length of water space. The gates at Keokuk, built exactly as are those at Panama, are higher, the lift here being forty feet as against thirty-two feet and twenty-eight feet in the Panama locks. The total lift Is greater at Panama, but there the locks are built In stairs of three flights. The lower gate of this lock is just like those at Panama, except that It is higher. The upper gate at Keokuk is of an entirely new type of- lock gate, being raised and lowered in a vertical runway by hydrolle power. Over the top of this gate when lowered runs the railroad track that will convey supplies to the power house in the river. Boats are passed through this new lock in from ten to fifteen minutes, as compared with two or three hours through the three locks of the old canal, to way nothing of the two hours slow going through the old canal itself. The running time for boats from above the rapids until clear of them will be shortened two or three hours by the new installation. The dry doc constructed here for the benefit of river boats Is the largest fresh water dry dock in in existence. Not the least of the engineering considerations at Keokuk was the annual battle with the ice. The ico fender built is more than half a mile long, constructed of solld concrete and connected with a floating boom of an additional reach of 500 feet. The fender is a bridge with plers of ten feet thick separating spans of sixty feet wide. The water will flow through the twenty-nine spans, while the Ice will be caught and stopped by the superstructure. The actual effect of this fender will be largely to stop the sudden breaking up of the ice floes, and to hold them until they naturally melt in their native beds. Of course the dam has transformed the rapids themselves into a huge pool, a lake, of many miles in length that is already the home and playground of scores of motor boats and other pleasure vessels. Around the shores of this lake, and along the top of the protecting sea wall, are being built the homes and summer cottages of this great engineering work. But of course, the main thing is the power house, which will be told of in tomorrow's letter. Tomorrow: THE KEOKUK DAM. III .- THE POWER PLANT.

headlines.title: Ponderous Personages
headlines.subtitle: CHRISTOPHER COLUMBUS.
headlines.fullText: By George Fitch, Author of "At Good Old Siwash." Christopher Columbus was born somewhere between 1430 and 1450, near Genoa, Several towns claim the honor of being his birthplace, and are a good deal more earnest about ft than they were when Christopher was a barefooted boy, with a ragged jacket and a poor wool-comber for a father. Columbus was given a careful education, mostly in mathematics and navigation, which, In the opinion of the neighbors, addled his mind, for he soon conceived the idea that the world was round. For many years he was regarded as the king of cranks as he rambled from court to court trying to convince credulous kings to lend him a fleet in which he might arrive in the far east by mailing west. If it had not boen for the sympathy and simple mindedness of a woman he would not have succeeded. Queen lsabella of Spain was so touched by his sadness and dejection that she prevailed on her husband to lend him three small vessels. This proves that woman should never be Intrusted with affairs of state. She is too credulous. Columbus salled from Palos, Aug. 2, 1432, with ninety men, each of whom had carefully made -his will, and after leaving the Canary islands steered southwest Into an unlimited supply of ocean which had never been navigated before. For centuries navigators had not ventured beyond the Canaries, but Columbus salled on for three weeks and even after his men had threatened him with death he continued to steer westward toward what the whole world Ermly believed to be the jumping off place. We land the intrepid explorers who have risked their lives to reach the north pole, far from a steam heater, but these men knew the north pole was there, whereas Columbus was the only man in his fleet who didn't wake with a start each night, believing the vessel was sliding off the edge of the world and beginning an eternal tumble among the other planets. Columbus was a great hero, but the other eighty-nine were even greater. They didn't even have the appearances to sustain them. Columbus discovered America October 12, 1492. That is, he began its discovery. Something new about the country is still being discovered dally. He was given tremendous honors for his daring and refrained with becoming dignity from saying "I told you so" to mankind when he returned, But he immediately plunged into American politics and became its first victim. After trying to run the new country for a few years he went home in chains and died in poverty later on, without even a postmastership to comfort him. Columbus is now one of history's greatest men, but if he had spent his early life arguing with wise men he would have died unknown and America might still be loafing around in an undiscovered and highly moral state. The foolish faith of woman, entirely unadulterated with logie and common sense, has mode more great men than any other one thing, (Copyrighted by George Mathew Adams.)

headlines.title: Little Dramas
headlines.fullText: BY GARRETT P. SERVISS. I wish I could persuade every. hody who is going to take a van. tion anywhere in the country the summer to become an amateur naturalist. You needn't natr books nor pursue the subject a. entifically nor systematically less you want to, but just simply keep your eyes and can ope. Leave your gun at home, legt it tempt you to murder some ins. cent creature, but take an open. glass in your pocket if contra- lent. The stage of nature is full et actors and actresses playing to. mas of absorbing interest and at. fering spectacles as fascinating and beautiful as str that ever the electric lighta of the theater shoes upon. The trouble with most people who seek rest of rece reation in the country is that they think only of automobile rides, tennis, golf, fishing and garden porties and neglect the delightful little sights that are an about them. Birds are really more Interesting thas golf balls, and the charm of a brook is not summet up in the trout that springs at your bait. If a list were made, not of the greatest but of the happiest, men who have lived it seems to me that Of. bert White of Selborne, would stand near the bad because of the delight that he found in simply watch. ing the little dramas of nature about him-a delleht which he succeeded in transmitting, through his wie. derful book, to many generations of his successos the earth. He wrote only that one little book, and Me talked only of birds, bees, wasps, fish, trees, storms, springs, ponds, glow-worms, squirrels-and ote alt tortoise-but what he wrote will be read with avidiy after a million novels have been forgotten. Consider only the pleasure the instruction, the philosophy and the delight that he got from that tortniet, which he made the most famous on turtle in all history ac-count, in the British National Museum. The tortoise turns up at unexpected junctures in many parts of the book. When you have got a little taste of his quality you look for him as you look for the appearances of Hamlet in the play. When the dandelions are starring the meadows, up comes that tortoise out of his mud bed. He Is covered with a sort of shell that could hear the weight of a cart wheel, and pet he pulls in his head and runs for shelter at the alight-est sprinkling of rain! A sultry March day brings Mim out, likt a hibernating fly buzalng round a Christmas hearth, but a touch of returning frost sends him scar-rying under ground. A golden day In November player another trick upon his instinct, and out he comes from the mud, hobbles to the feet of a good old lady whom he has known for thirty years. But he is only a youth yet. for the chelonian spas of life covers a century or more, and If he were eldre perhaps he would be wiser, and not let the vagarlet of the season fool him. Gilbert White studien all hin movements, bets at all his secreta, admires his sagacity, and wonders at hà sagacity, ami wonders at his deliberate movements when he begins to dig his winter bed In a swamp, for, "the motion of its legs Is ridiculously slow, little et. ceeding the hour-hand of a clock!" White made the acquaintance of this tortolse at z friend's him house in Sussex, and whenever he went there he bought him, dug him out of his hibernaculum et winter dormitory, hissing with anger at being de turbed, and carried him in a box of earth, eighty sih by postchaises, and put him in his own garde #: does one's heart good to see how delighted the old sil uralist was with his bargain. He thought as much of him as you might of a pet dog-perhaps mitt Then began a little life drama before an appredu tive spectator. To the reader's regret, Gilbert White giver car brief glimpses of it in his book. You see the tortol sitting comfortably under a cabbage leaf when to sun is too hot in midsummer; you catch a sight of him careened up against a southward-facing wall # early autumn to catch every feeble ray of warmat on his back, and, finally you see him stealing out of the garden by back ways, eluding the eyes of the gardener and of his master, to keep a tryst in sim neighbor's field, for Cupld'a darts could penetrate ent his hard shell! Everybody might be a Gilbert White in a small wir, and It is certain that anybody who tries to be will fit the world a pleasanter place to live in and his mil richer for the experience,

headlines.title: A Proposition That Should Appeal to All
headlines.fullText: The suggestion that the state of Texas provide for the payment of Its treasury warrants at their face value is one that should appeal to every fair minded person. As business proposition it cannot well be opposed. It is a plain statement that the state should do just as other creditors do dally in business affairs, pay the Interest on the money necessary to meet its honest obligationa. There is Do fairness in compelling the state creditors and employes to disceent the warrants for the money due them. The : - - not agree to pay ita employes four per cel: ica than the salaries at which they were employed, and it did not contract to pay the men who have supplied it four per cent less than the specified price. Being without funds it should do as the individual would have to do, obtain the fund and pay the interest upon it until the time the treasury is in condition to take up the obligations. It Is not flattering to the prestige of the state to hae ita I. O. U.'s hawked about at discount. No fair minded eltizen will port contend that the who have given their of contracts with the state should be the ones to bear the burden of the state's financial embarrassment. It is the state's load, and the state should carry it. State purchasing is done on a system of competitive bidding. By that means each vendor in pitted against the other as to prices. Quality being equal, the man who cuts the price the lowest gets the award. It can thus be readily seen that in instances the difference between a cash payment at the time due and payment with a warrant that can be transferred into cash only at a discount, may make the difference between a contract upon which a profit is made and one resulting in loss. The state certainly does not want to defraud Its citizens. It is dangerously near that position if honorable and legitimate means are at hand to pay its debts, and it does not adopt auch means, If the state should deliberately strike four per cent from all its liabilities, the action would be denounced from border to horder. When creditors are forced needlessly to discount their warrants that much, there is little to distinguish the course. If the situation cannot be helped there is nothing to do but make the best of it, but it is reported that the banks are willing to cash state treasury warrants at their face value and charge the state Interest on such warrants so paid from the time each warrant is paid by the bank until the state treasury has funds to redeem it. As these warrants are practically sure to be palti within eight months, a banking arrangement of this sort at six per cent would in effect cost the state just exactly what the creditors are now paying for the state by discounting their warrants. That the burden should be shifted to the state where it belongs seems but reasonable.

headlines.title: Hail the "Silage Special!"
headlines.fullText: Beginning Aug. 11, the Rock Island railroad will carry the gospel of the silo to farmers in the Panhandle, Eastern New Mexico, Oklahoma and North Texas. The slio is the farm stocking-it yields the money to the enterprising and thrifty. Texas railroads never sleep, They never wait for "tips." They see what is to be done-and do It. The "Silage Special" is Twentieth century farm helpfulness. Let Texas grow.

headlines.title: EARTH MOON IN 49 HOURS.
headlines.fullText: Robert Esnault Pelterie, one of the best known engineers in France, has just amazed the scientifie world by describing an apparatus by means of which it would be possible to make a trip to the moon in two days. Mr. Pelterie has no such project in his head at present, but merely desires to demonstrate that auch a trip as Jules Verne imagined is scientifically possible, though not by exactly the same means as those described by the novelist. He says that an extremely light vehicle propelled by a motor or tremendous power, not operating propellers, for the reason that there is no atmosphere on which they could work, but operating a discharge like that of a skyrocket on a gigantic scale, would be able to pass out beyond the sphere of the gravitation of the carth and reach the moon's attraction, when it would drop of its own weight upon the surface of our satellite, Mr. Pelterie has calculated that a projectile weighing one ton would need a motor developing 414,000 horse power. The journey would have three stages, The first of these, the passage from the earth to that point at which its attraction censes, would occupy twenty-four minutes and nine seconds. The second stage would be crossing the neutral zone wherein there is neither attraction by the earth nor by the moon, and this, he says, would be accomplished by the inertia of the projectile, there being nothing to check the speed at which it entered the zone. For this stage, of the journey forty-eight hours and fifty minutes would be needed. The final stage, the dropping to the surface of the moon, would take three minutes and forty-six seconds. Thus the total length of the trip would be forty-nine hours, seventeen minutes and fifty-five seconds. Most astronomers and mathematicians would tell Mr. Peleterie that his calculations are wrong, for the reason that there is no neutral zone in which neither carth mor moon attracts. The attraction extends throughout space, diminishing, of course, with the distance. The further away from the earth one went the less strength would its attraction have, and the nearer one enme to the moon the greater strength would its attraction have .- Kansas City Journal.

headlines.title: THE MIGHTY Y. M. C. A.
headlines.fullText: Three score years ago T. V. Sullivan, a sea captain, organized in Boston a new business, modeled after something he had heard existed In England. It was different from any American business then in existence. The new venture began in a very small way. with practically no capital and no backing. As it became a demonstrated success, capital came to it and men of affairs became interested in its management. Today this organization has thousands of employes, It has 2,196 offices in almost as many American cities. Ita expenses are more than $10,000,000 a year. It has one in New York costing $400,000 and another in Cleveland valued at 4953,000. It Is building an office building in Atlanta at a cost of $442,900, another in Philadelphia valued at $587,000, and a third in Boston at an expenditure of 31.200,000. For five years this corporation has been erecting branch bouses at the rato of one every elx days. All told, it owna 125 buildings. Its real estate is worth $70.000.000, and It has maintenance funds of $14,000.000 more, giving it a total capital of $81,000,000. The name of this organization is the Young Men's Christian Association. Ils business is the conservation of America's manhood .- World's Work.

headlines.title: The Optimist
headlines.subtitle: THE WATER OF LIFE.
headlines.fullText: BY REV. J. P. HICKS. "But whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst; but the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water, springing up au everlasting life."-John 4:16 Water is one of the most important things in our life. Then can be no life without want The crops soon parch and without the beautiful rain wiki fills everything with new viele The Great Teacher of the Ap uses water to represent salvation. Water saves the crops from burning and the health of the people. I1 always, or most always, great summer resorts it is the clear sparkling water which Is the source attraction. The people drink the water unsparinas We could hardly live a day without good cold walm There is nothing that causes such fatigue and soon as a lack of cold water. The body burns without it. After working hard on a hot day, there is nothiss that fills one with so much new vigor as to get good ly thirsty, draught the of best cold prepared water. food When does one is exceeding" web not taste To enjoy the food, there must be the absence burning thirst. This can only be driven away by cold water. But when we drink of the everflowle water, however cool it may be, the thirst retard again soon. But the water of salvation quetche the thirat continuously. The thirsty life is the disde isfied life. The world is full of dissatisfaction. . Dle grow weary end dissatisfied with their sufreune Ings and want to go somewhere else and engage other work. This is the spirit of dissatisfaction. They Is no better evidence of the fact that we have sever quenched our thirst at salvation's well than ever present spirit of dissatisfaction. The Apot Paul, who had quenched his thirst at the ever flow-ing well of salvation, sald: "I have learned in what ever state I am therewith to be content."
